title: Reverse array in place
description: Reverse the elements of an array in place

examples.explanation: Step 1: Input array = [10, 20, 30], n = 3. Step 2: Create temp array = [0, 0, 0]. Step 3: Reverse copy: i=0, temp[0] = arr[2] = 30 → temp = [30, 0, 0]; i=1, temp[1] = arr[1] = 20 → temp = [30, 20, 0]; i=2, temp[2] = arr[0] = 10 → temp = [30, 20, 10]. Step 4: Copy back: i=0, arr[0] = temp[0] = 30 → arr = [30, 20, 30]; i=1, arr[1] = temp[1] = 20 → arr = [30, 20, 30]; i=2, arr[2] = temp[2] = 10 → arr = [30, 20, 10]. Step 5: Output = [30, 20, 10].

approaches.name: Brute Force with Extra Space O(n²)
approaches.summary: Create a new array and copy elements from the original in reverse order, then copy back to the original array. This uses extra space and is inefficient due to double copying.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Reverse Array In Place Using Brute Force with Extra Space O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete solution that creates a new array, copies elements in reverse order, then copies back to original array.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the reverse array problem using a brute force method with extra space. The intuition is straightforward: we need to reverse the order of elements, so we can create a new array, fill it with elements from the original array in reverse order, and then copy everything back. This works because arrays allow direct indexing, making it easy to access elements from the end. The algorithm has three main phases: 1) Create a new empty array of the same size as the original. 2) Iterate through the original array from the end to the beginning, copying each element to the new array from the beginning. 3) Copy all elements from the new array back to the original array. The time complexity is O(n) because we make two passes through n elements (one for reverse copying, one for copying back). The space complexity is O(n) for the new array. This connects to the parent problem by providing a complete, albeit inefficient, solution. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle empty arrays, off-by-one errors in indexing, and not properly copying back to the original array. Real code pattern: def reverse_array(arr): n = len(arr); temp = [0]*n; for i in range(n): temp[i] = arr[n-1-i]; for i in range(n): arr[i] = temp[i]; return arr.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input array = [10, 20, 30], n = 3. Step 2: Create temp array = [0, 0, 0]. Step 3: Reverse copy: i=0, temp[0] = arr[2] = 30 → temp = [30, 0, 0]; i=1, temp[1] = arr[1] = 20 → temp = [30, 20, 0]; i=2, temp[2] = arr[0] = 10 → temp = [30, 20, 10]. Step 4: Copy back: i=0, arr[0] = temp[0] = 30 → arr = [30, 20, 30]; i=1, arr[1] = temp[1] = 20 → arr = [30, 20, 30]; i=2, arr[2] = temp[2] = 10 → arr = [30, 20, 10]. Step 5: Output = [30, 20, 10].

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Copy Elements from Original Array to New Array in Reverse Order
ladders.desc: Create a new array and populate it with elements from the original array in reverse sequence.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach, responsible for reversing the order of elements into a temporary array. The intuition is that to reverse an array, we need to access elements from the end and place them at the beginning of a new array. This works because arrays support direct indexing, allowing us to compute positions mathematically. The algorithm: 1) Determine the length n of the original array. 2) Create a new array temp of size n. 3) For each index i from 0 to n-1, set temp[i] = arr[n-1-i]. This copies the last element of arr to the first position of temp, the second-last to the second, and so on. Time complexity is O(n) as we iterate through n elements once. Space complexity is O(n) for the temp array. This feeds into the parent problem by producing the reversed array, which then needs to be copied back. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., using n-i instead of n-1-i), not handling empty arrays, and creating temp with wrong size. Real code pattern: def reverse_copy(arr): n = len(arr); temp = [0]*n; for i in range(n): temp[i] = arr[n-1-i]; return temp.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input array = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], n = 5. Step 2: Create temp = [0, 0, 0, 0, 0]. Step 3: For i=0: temp[0] = arr[4] = 5 → temp = [5, 0, 0, 0, 0]. Step 4: For i=1: temp[1] = arr[3] = 4 → temp = [5, 4, 0, 0, 0]. Step 5: For i=2: temp[2] = arr[2] = 3 → temp = [5, 4, 3, 0, 0]. Step 6: For i=3: temp[3] = arr[1] = 2 → temp = [5, 4, 3, 2, 0]. Step 7: For i=4: temp[4] = arr[0] = 1 → temp = [5, 4, 3, 2, 1]. Step 8: Output temp = [5, 4, 3, 2, 1].

Step 1: Input array = [10, 20, 30], n = 3. Step 2: Create temp = [0, 0, 0]. Step 3: For i=0: temp[0] = arr[2] = 30 → temp = [30, 0, 0]. Step 4: For i=1: temp[1] = arr[1] = 20 → temp = [30, 20, 0]. Step 5: For i=2: temp[2] = arr[0] = 10 → temp = [30, 20, 10]. Step 6: Output temp = [30, 20, 10].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Access Array Elements in Reverse Order Using Index Calculation
ladders.desc: Compute indices to retrieve elements from the end of an array sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves the technique of accessing array elements in reverse order by calculating indices mathematically. The intuition is that to reverse an array, we need to start from the last element and move towards the first, which can be done by computing the index as (n-1-i) for the i-th position in the new order. This works because arrays are zero-indexed: the last element is at index n-1, the second-last at n-2, etc. The algorithm: Given an array arr of length n, for i from 0 to n-1, access arr[n-1-i]. This yields elements in reverse sequence. Time complexity is O(1) per access, O(n) overall if iterating. Space complexity is O(1) as no extra data structures are used. This feeds into the sub-routine by providing the method to populate the temp array. Common mistakes include forgetting the -1 in n-1-i (leading to index out of bounds), miscomputing for empty arrays, and not validating input. Real code pattern: for i in range(n): element = arr[n-1-i].

examples.trace: Step 1: Input array = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], n = 5. Step 2: For i=0: index = 5-1-0 = 4, element = arr[4] = 5. Step 3: For i=1: index = 5-1-1 = 3, element = arr[3] = 4. Step 4: For i=2: index = 5-1-2 = 2, element = arr[2] = 3. Step 5: For i=3: index = 5-1-3 = 1, element = arr[1] = 2. Step 6: For i=4: index = 5-1-4 = 0, element = arr[0] = 1. Step 7: Output sequence = 5, 4, 3, 2, 1.

Step 1: Input array = [10, 20, 30], n = 3. Step 2: For i=0: index = 3-1-0 = 2, element = arr[2] = 30. Step 3: For i=1: index = 3-1-1 = 1, element = arr[1] = 20. Step 4: For i=2: index = 3-1-2 = 0, element = arr[0] = 10. Step 5: Output sequence = 30, 20, 10.

ladders.title: Building Block — Create a New Array of Same Size and Initialize with Default Values
ladders.desc: Allocate a new array with identical length to the original and set all elements to a default value.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves creating a new array that mirrors the size of an existing array, which is necessary for storing reversed elements temporarily. The intuition is that to copy data, we need a destination container of appropriate capacity. This works because arrays have fixed sizes, so we must pre-allocate based on the original length. The algorithm: 1) Determine the length n of the original array. 2) Create a new array temp of size n. 3) Initialize all elements of temp to a default value (e.g., 0 or None). Time complexity is O(n) for initialization if setting each element, but can be O(1) if the language handles it. Space complexity is O(n) for the new array. This feeds into the core logic by providing the temp array to hold reversed elements. Common mistakes include creating temp with wrong size (e.g., n-1), not initializing (leading to undefined values), and using mutable defaults incorrectly. Real code pattern: n = len(arr); temp = [0]*n.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input original array length n = 5. Step 2: Create new array temp with size 5. Step 3: Initialize each element to 0: temp[0] = 0, temp[1] = 0, temp[2] = 0, temp[3] = 0, temp[4] = 0. Step 4: Output temp = [0, 0, 0, 0, 0].
Step 1: Input original array length n = 3. Step 2: Create new array temp with size 3. Step 3: Initialize each element to 0: temp[0] = 0, temp[1] = 0, temp[2] = 0. Step 4: Output temp = [0, 0, 0].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access an Array Element by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve the value stored at a specific position in an array using its index.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves accessing an element in an array given its index. The intuition is that arrays are contiguous blocks of memory where each element has a unique position (index), starting from 0. This works because the array's base address plus the index times element size gives the memory location. The algorithm: Given an array arr and an index i, return arr[i]. Time complexity is O(1) as it's a direct memory access. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the building block by allowing retrieval of elements for copying. Common mistakes include using invalid indices (negative or >= array length), not checking bounds, and confusing zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: element = arr[i].

examples.trace: Step 1: Input array = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]. Step 2: Index i = 2. Step 3: Access arr[2]. Step 4: The element at position 2 is 3 (since indices: 0→1, 1→2, 2→3). Step 5: Output = 3.
Step 1: Input array = [10, 20, 30]. Step 2: Index i = 0. Step 3: Access arr[0]. Step 4: The element at position 0 is 10. Step 5: Output = 10.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand What an Array Is and How It Stores Elements
ladders.desc: Learn that an array is a collection of items stored in contiguous memory locations, accessible by indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding what an array is. An array is a data structure that holds a fixed number of elements of the same type in contiguous memory locations. The intuition is that it's like a row of boxes, each with a unique number (index) starting from 0. This works because the computer can quickly calculate the memory address of any element using the base address and index. Key properties: 1) Fixed size (determined at creation). 2) Zero-based indexing (first element at index 0). 3) Contiguous storage (elements are next to each other in memory). Time and space analysis isn't applicable here as it's a conceptual foundation. This feeds into basic operations by providing the context for accessing elements. Common mistakes include thinking indices start at 1, not understanding fixed size, and confusing arrays with lists (in some languages). Real code pattern: In Python, an array-like structure is a list, but for this exercise, we treat it as an array with fixed operations.

examples.trace: Step 1: Consider an array with elements 5, 10, 15. Step 2: It has 3 elements, so size = 3. Step 3: The first element is at index 0: value = 5. Step 4: The second element is at index 1: value = 10. Step 5: The third element is at index 2: value = 15. Step 6: Output mapping: index 0 → 5, index 1 → 10, index 2 → 15.

Step 1: Consider an array with elements 100, 200. Step 2: It has 2 elements, so size = 2. Step 3: The first element is at index 0: value = 100. Step 4: The second element is at index 1: value = 200. Step 5: Output mapping: index 0 → 100, index 1 → 200.

approaches.name: Two Pointer Swap O(n)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers starting at the beginning and end of the array, swap elements, and move pointers inward until they meet. This reverses the array in place efficiently.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Reverse Array In Place Using Two Pointer Swap
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of reversing an array in place using two pointers that swap elements while moving inward.
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires reversing an array in place using the two-pointer swap approach. This means we must modify the original array without creating a new one, achieving O(n) time and O(1) space. The intuition is that reversing an array involves swapping symmetric elements: the first with the last, second with second-last, etc., until we reach the middle. We use two pointers: one starting at index 0 (left) and one at the last index (right). In each iteration, we swap the elements at these pointers, then increment left and decrement right. The process stops when left >= right, as crossing would re-swap elements back to their original positions. This is efficient because each element is visited once, and swaps are constant time. The algorithm: 1. Initialize left = 0, right = n-1 where n is array length. 2. While left < right: a. Swap array[left] and array[right]. b. left++, right--. 3. Return the array (though in-place modification means the original is reversed). Time complexity is O(n) since we iterate through half the array (n/2 swaps), and space is O(1) as we only use a few variables. Connection to L1: This builds on the key subroutine of swapping two elements, which is the core action here. Common mistakes include: not handling empty or single-element arrays (which work fine as the loop doesn't run), off-by-one errors in pointer bounds (use < not <= to avoid double-swapping the middle element in odd-length arrays), and modifying pointers incorrectly. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def reverse_array(arr): left, right = 0, len(arr)-1; while left < right: arr[left], arr[right] = arr[right], arr[left]; left += 1; right -= 1; return arr.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=4, arr=[1,2,3,4,5]; swap arr[0] and arr[4] -> arr=[5,2,3,4,1]; left=1, right=3. Step 2: left=1, right=3, arr=[5,2,3,4,1]; swap arr[1] and arr[3] -> arr=[5,4,3,2,1]; left=2, right=2. Step 3: left=2, right=2, condition left < right is false, stop. Final arr=[5,4,3,2,1].

Step 1: left=0, right=3, arr=['a','b','c','d']; swap arr[0] and arr[3] -> arr=['d','b','c','a']; left=1, right=2. Step 2: left=1, right=2, arr=['d','b','c','a']; swap arr[1] and arr[2] -> arr=['d','c','b','a']; left=2, right=1. Step 3: left=2, right=1, condition left < right is false, stop. Final arr=['d','c','b','a'].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Swap Two Elements in an Array
ladders.desc: Implement a function to swap two elements at given indices in an array.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the most important helper for the two-pointer approach: swapping two elements in an array. The intuition is that to reverse an array, we need to exchange pairs of elements, and swapping is the fundamental operation that enables this. We take an array and two indices i and j, then interchange the values at these positions. The algorithm: 1. Check if indices are valid (0 <= i, j < length of array). 2. Use a temporary variable to hold one value during the swap to avoid overwriting. 3. Assign array[i] to temp, array[j] to array[i], and temp to array[j]. This ensures both values are correctly exchanged. Time complexity is O(1) as it involves constant-time assignments, and space is O(1) for the temp variable. Connection to L0: This swap function is called repeatedly in the main loop to reverse the array. Common mistakes include: not using a temp variable (which leads to losing one value), swapping with invalid indices (should handle or assume valid input), and modifying the array incorrectly. Real code pattern: def swap(arr, i, j): temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp; return arr.

examples.trace: Step 1: arr=['a','b','c'], i=1, j=2. Step 2: temp = arr[1] = 'b'. Step 3: arr[1] = arr[2] = 'c' -> arr=['a','c','c']. Step 4: arr[2] = temp = 'b' -> arr=['a','c','b']. Final arr=['a','c','b'].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate with Two Pointers Until They Meet
ladders.desc: Implement the loop that moves two pointers inward from the start and end of an array until they cross or meet.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves controlling the two-pointer movement: starting one pointer at the beginning and another at the end of an array, then moving them inward until they meet or cross. The intuition is that to process symmetric pairs in an array (like for reversal), we need to traverse from both ends simultaneously, and the loop condition ensures we stop at the right point to avoid over-processing. The algorithm: 1. Initialize left = 0 and right = n-1, where n is array length. 2. Use a while loop with condition left < right. 3. In each iteration, perform some operation (e.g., swap, as in L1), then increment left and decrement right. This ensures we process each pair once and stop when pointers meet (for even n) or cross (for odd n, where middle element stays in place). Time complexity depends on the operation inside the loop, but the pointer movement itself is O(n) in the worst case. Space is O(1) for the pointers. Connection to L1: This loop structure calls the swap function at each step to achieve reversal. Common mistakes include: using <= instead of < (which causes double-processing of the middle element in odd-length arrays), not updating pointers correctly, or infinite loops if pointers don't converge. Real code pattern: left, right = 0, len(arr)-1; while left < right: # do something with arr[left] and arr[right]; left += 1; right -= 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=4, condition left<right true. Step 2: left=1, right=3, condition true. Step 3: left=2, right=2, condition false (left < right is false). Stop. Pointers met at index 2.
Step 1: left=0, right=3, condition true. Step 2: left=1, right=2, condition true. Step 3: left=2, right=1, condition false (left < right is false). Stop. Pointers crossed after left=2, right=1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Access and Modify Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Practice reading from and writing to specific positions in an array using indices.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on accessing and modifying elements in an array using their indices, which is essential for the swap operation in the two-pointer approach. The intuition is that arrays are indexed collections, and to manipulate them (like reversing), we need to reliably read values from and assign values to specific positions. The algorithm involves: 1. Access an element at index i using array[i] to read its value. 2. Modify an element at index i by assigning a new value: array[i] = new_value. This is fundamental because swapping requires both reading and writing at two indices. Time complexity for access and modification is O(1) per operation, and space is O(1) as no extra data structures are needed. Connection to L2: This skill is used within the loop to get and set values during pointer movement. Common mistakes include: using out-of-bounds indices (which cause errors), confusing zero-based indexing, or not understanding that assignment changes the array in place. Real code pattern: value = arr[index] to read; arr[index] = value to write.

examples.input: arr=[10,20,30], read index=1, write index=0 with value=50
examples.trace: Step 1: Read arr[1] = 20. Step 2: Write arr[0] = 50 -> arr becomes [50,20,30]. Final arr=[50,20,30].

examples.input: arr=['a','b','c'], read index=2, write index=1 with value='z'
examples.trace: Step 1: Read arr[2] = 'c'. Step 2: Write arr[1] = 'z' -> arr becomes ['a','z','c']. Final arr=['a','z','c'].

testCases.input: arr=[1,2,3], read index=0, write index=2 with value=9
arr=[100], read index=0, write index=0 with value=200
arr=[], read index=0, write index=0 with value=5
testCases.expected: [] (error if out of bounds, but assume valid)

testCases.input: arr=[5,10,15,20], read index=3, write index=1 with value=25
arr=['x','y'], read index=1, write index=0 with value='z'
arr=[3.14, 2.71], read index=1, write index=0 with value=1.41
arr=[true, false], read index=0, write index=1 with value=true
arr=[10,20,30,40,50], read index=4, write index=2 with value=60
arr=['cat','dog','bird'], read index=2, write index=0 with value='fish'
arr=[7,8,9], read index=1, write index=1 with value=10

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Use a Temporary Variable for Value Exchange
ladders.desc: Learn to exchange the values of two variables using a temporary holder.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation teaches how to swap the values of two variables using a temporary variable, a fundamental skill needed for the array swap in the two-pointer approach. The intuition is that when swapping two values, if we assign one directly to the other, we lose the original value; a temporary variable acts as a safe placeholder to prevent this loss. The algorithm: 1. Let a and b be two variables. 2. Assign temp = a to store the value of a. 3. Assign a = b to set a to b's value. 4. Assign b = temp to set b to the original value of a (stored in temp). This ensures both values are exchanged correctly. Time complexity is O(1) as it involves three constant-time assignments, and space is O(1) for the temp variable. Connection to L3: This operation is used when modifying array elements during a swap. Common mistakes include: skipping the temp variable (e.g., a = b; b = a, which makes both equal to b's original value), or using the wrong order. Real code pattern: temp = a; a = b; b = temp.

examples.trace: Step 1: temp = a = 5. Step 2: a = b = 10 -> a=10. Step 3: b = temp = 5 -> b=5. Final a=10, b=5.
Step 1: temp = a = 'hello'. Step 2: a = b = 'world' -> a='world'. Step 3: b = temp = 'hello' -> b='hello'. Final a='world', b='hello'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Array Indexing and Zero-Based Counting
ladders.desc: Learn how arrays are indexed starting from 0 and how to access positions using integers.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers the basics of array indexing, specifically that arrays use zero-based counting: the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. This is crucial for the two-pointer approach because we need to correctly identify start and end positions. The intuition is that indexing allows us to pinpoint specific elements in a collection, and zero-based indexing is a standard in many programming languages that simplifies calculations (e.g., last index is length-1). The algorithm involves: 1. Given an array of length n, the indices range from 0 to n-1. 2. To access an element, use array[index] where index is an integer within this range. 3. The last element is at index n-1, not n. Time and space are not applicable here as it's a conceptual understanding. Connection to L4: This knowledge is needed to set up pointers and perform swaps at correct indices. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors (e.g., using index n for the last element), confusing with one-based indexing, or not handling empty arrays (length 0 means no indices). Real code pattern: arr = [10,20,30]; first_element = arr[0]; last_element = arr[len(arr)-1].

examples.input: arr=[5,10,15], find index of first and last elements
examples.output: first at index 0, last at index 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Array has length 3, indices are 0,1,2. Step 2: First element is 5 at index 0. Step 3: Last element is 15 at index 2 (since 3-1=2).
Step 1: Array indices: 0='a', 1='b', 2='c', 3='d'. Step 2: Element at index 2 is 'c'.

approaches.name: Recursive Swap O(n)
approaches.summary: Recursively swap the first and last elements of the array, reducing the problem size with each call. This uses the call stack for reversal.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Reverse Array In Place Using Recursive Swap
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution that reverses an array by swapping first and last elements, then recursively processing the inner subarray.
ladders.explanation: This approach reverses an array in place using recursion. The intuition is that reversing an entire array can be broken down into: 1) swapping the first and last elements, and 2) recursively reversing the remaining inner subarray. This creates a divide-and-conquer pattern where each recursive call handles a smaller portion of the array. The algorithm works by maintaining two pointers (start and end) that move toward the center. At each step, we swap elements at these positions, then recursively call the function with start+1 and end-1. The base case occurs when start >= end, meaning we've processed all elements (for even length) or reached the middle element (for odd length). Time complexity is O(n) since each element is visited once for swapping. Space complexity is O(n) due to the recursion stack depth, which equals n/2 in the worst case. This connects to the key subroutine (L1) by implementing the complete recursive reversal logic. Common mistakes include: forgetting base case (causing infinite recursion), incorrect pointer updates, or modifying array bounds incorrectly. Real code pattern: def reverse_recursive(arr, start, end): if start >= end: return; arr[start], arr[end] = arr[end], arr[start]; reverse_recursive(arr, start+1, end-1).

examples.trace: Initial: arr=[1,2,3,4,5], start=0, end=4. Call 1: swap arr[0] and arr[4] → [5,2,3,4,1]; recursive call with start=1, end=3. Call 2: swap arr[1] and arr[3] → [5,4,3,2,1]; recursive call with start=2, end=2. Call 3: start=2, end=2, start>=end, base case reached, return. Final: [5,4,3,2,1].

Initial: arr=['a','b','c','d'], start=0, end=3. Call 1: swap arr[0] and arr[3] → ['d','b','c','a']; recursive call with start=1, end=2. Call 2: swap arr[1] and arr[2] → ['d','c','b','a']; recursive call with start=2, end=1. Call 3: start=2, end=1, start>=end, base case reached, return. Final: ['d','c','b','a'].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Swap Function with Pointers
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that swaps elements at given indices and recursively calls itself on the inner subarray.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that performs the swapping and recursion. The intuition is that reversing any subarray can be achieved by swapping its endpoints and delegating the inner portion to a recursive call. The function takes an array and two indices (start and end) as parameters. It first checks if start >= end (base case), which means the subarray has zero or one element (already reversed). If not, it swaps the elements at start and end positions, then makes a recursive call with start+1 and end-1 to process the remaining inner subarray. This creates a chain of recursive calls that gradually move the pointers toward the center. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the size of the subarray (end-start+1), as each element is visited once. Space complexity is O(k) for the recursion stack. This feeds into L0 by being the main function called to reverse the entire array (with start=0, end=n-1). Common mistakes: incorrect base condition (using start==end only misses empty subarrays), not updating pointers correctly in recursive call, or modifying array outside the given bounds. Real code pattern: def recursive_swap(arr, start, end): if start >= end: return; arr[start], arr[end] = arr[end], arr[start]; recursive_swap(arr, start+1, end-1).

examples.trace: Call 1: start=0, end=3, swap arr[0] and arr[3] → [4,2,3,1]; recursive call with start=1, end=2. Call 2: start=1, end=2, swap arr[1] and arr[2] → [4,3,2,1]; recursive call with start=2, end=1. Call 3: start=2, end=1, start>=end, base case, return. Final: [4,3,2,1].
Call 1: start=1, end=2, swap arr[1] and arr[2] → [5,7,6]; recursive call with start=2, end=1. Call 2: start=2, end=1, start>=end, base case, return. Final: [5,7,6].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Swapping Endpoints and Recursing on Inner Subarray
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the pattern of swapping first/last elements and recursively processing the remaining middle portion.
ladders.explanation: This core logic captures the essential recursive strategy: to reverse a list, swap the outermost elements, then recursively reverse the inner sublist. The intuition is based on mathematical induction: if you can reverse a smaller list, you can reverse a larger one by handling the endpoints and delegating the middle. The algorithm for a subarray from index 'start' to 'end' is: 1) If the subarray has 0 or 1 elements (start >= end), it's already reversed (base case). 2) Otherwise, swap the elements at positions start and end. 3) Recursively apply the same logic to the subarray from start+1 to end-1. This reduces the problem size by 2 with each recursive call. Time complexity is O(n) for an n-element array, as each swap takes O(1) and there are n/2 swaps. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. This connects to L1 by being the algorithmic pattern implemented in the recursive function. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle empty or single-element cases, incorrect recursion parameters, or attempting to swap when start > end. Real code pattern: if start >= end: return; swap(arr[start], arr[end]); recurse on (start+1, end-1).

examples.trace: Step 1: Swap arr[1]=2 and arr[3]=4 → [1,4,3,2,5]. Step 2: Recursively process inner subarray from start=2 to end=2 (single element [3], base case). Final: [1,4,3,2,5].
Step 1: Swap arr[1]='b' and arr[2]='c' → ['a','c','b','d']. Step 2: Recursively process inner subarray from start=2 to end=1 (empty, base case). Final: ['a','c','b','d'].

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Function Calls with Array Modification
ladders.desc: Practice writing recursive functions that modify arrays in place by passing indices as parameters.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the mechanics of recursion with array manipulation. The intuition is that recursion allows solving a problem by breaking it into smaller instances of the same problem, and for arrays, we often use indices to define subproblems. Here, we write a recursive function that takes an array and two indices, modifies the array based on those indices, and makes a recursive call with updated indices. The key aspects are: 1) Defining a base case that stops recursion (e.g., when indices cross). 2) Performing an operation (like swapping) on the current indices. 3) Making a recursive call with adjusted indices to process the next subproblem. This teaches how recursion can be used for in-place array algorithms. Time complexity depends on the number of recursive calls; here it's O(n) for n elements. Space complexity is O(n) due to call stack. This connects to L2 by providing the recursive framework for the swap-and-recurse pattern. Common mistakes: not passing arrays by reference (in languages that require it), infinite recursion from missing base case, or incorrect index arithmetic. Real code pattern: def recursive_modify(arr, i, j): if base_condition: return; perform_operation(arr, i, j); recursive_modify(arr, i+delta, j-delta).

examples.input: Function that sets arr[i] to 0 and recurses: arr=[1,2,3,4], i=0, j=3
examples.trace: Call 1: i=0, j=3, set arr[0]=0 → [0,2,3,4]; recurse with i=1, j=2. Call 2: i=1, j=2, set arr[1]=0 → [0,0,3,4]; recurse with i=2, j=1. Call 3: i=2, j=1, i>j, base case, return. Final: [0,0,3,4] (note: not all set due to base condition i>j).

examples.input: Function that swaps arr[i] and arr[j] and recurses: arr=[5,6,7], i=0, j=2
examples.trace: Call 1: i=0, j=2, swap arr[0] and arr[2] → [7,6,5]; recurse with i=1, j=1. Call 2: i=1, j=1, base case (i>=j), return. Final: [7,6,5].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Swapping Two Elements in an Array
ladders.desc: Learn how to swap two elements in an array using a temporary variable or tuple unpacking.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is fundamental to many algorithms, including the recursive swap approach. Swapping two elements in an array means exchanging their values at specified indices. The intuition is simple: to swap A and B, you need a temporary holder for one value while moving the other. The standard method uses a temporary variable: temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp. In Python-style, tuple unpacking allows a one-liner: arr[i], arr[j] = arr[j], arr[i]. This works because the right side is evaluated first, creating a tuple of the values, then assigned to the left side. Time complexity is O(1) as it involves constant-time assignments. Space complexity is O(1) for the temporary variable (or none for tuple unpacking). This connects to L3 as the operation performed in each recursive step. Common mistakes: trying to swap without a temporary (e.g., arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = arr[i] loses the original arr[i]), using incorrect indices, or assuming swap works for immutable types. Real code pattern: temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp; OR arr[i], arr[j] = arr[j], arr[i].

examples.trace: Step 1: Store arr[0]=10 in temp. Step 2: Set arr[0] to arr[2]=30 → [30,20,30]. Step 3: Set arr[2] to temp=10 → [30,20,10]. Final: [30,20,10].
Using tuple unpacking: Evaluate right side: (arr[2], arr[1]) = ('c','b'). Assign to left: arr[1]='c', arr[2]='b' → ['a','c','b']. Final: ['a','c','b'].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Accessing and Modifying Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Understand how to read and write values at specific positions in an array using indices.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept is essential for any array manipulation. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, accessible via indices (usually starting at 0). The intuition is that each position in the array has a unique index, allowing direct access to its value. To read an element, use arr[index] to retrieve its value. To modify an element, use arr[index] = new_value to assign a new value. Indices must be within the valid range [0, length-1]; going outside causes errors. This is the building block for operations like swapping, as it enables reading from and writing to specific positions. Time complexity for access/modification is O(1) due to direct indexing. Space complexity is O(1) for the operation itself. This connects to L4 by providing the basic skills needed to perform swaps. Common mistakes: using 1-based indexing (assuming first element is at index 1), accessing out-of-bounds indices, or confusing index with value. Real code pattern: value = arr[i]; arr[j] = value; OR arr[i] = new_value.

examples.input: arr=[10,20,30], read index 1, write index 2 with value 40
examples.trace: Step 1: Read arr[1] = 20. Step 2: Write arr[2] = 40, changing arr from [10,20,30] to [10,20,40]. Final: [10,20,40].

examples.input: arr=['a','b','c'], read index 0, write index 1 with value 'd'
examples.trace: Step 1: Read arr[0] = 'a'. Step 2: Write arr[1] = 'd', changing arr from ['a','b','c'] to ['a','d','c']. Final: ['a','d','c'].
